video_title: Is The Rally Over? Has The Pullback Now Started? | Lance Roberts
gist: After 9-10 straight up weeks, Lance Roberts says a correction has technically begun (sell signal triggered, 20-day MA broken) but argues it will likely be a shallow 5-7% rotational pullback — capital sloshing from overbought semis/mega-cap tech into lagging value/quality/dividend names rather than leaving the market — unless an oil-supply spike, liquidity tightening, or impaired hyperscaler capex hits forward earnings. Macro backdrop is resilient (capex + deficits + bonus-depreciation juicing growth, earnings revisions surging like a post-recession recovery), but late-cycle warning signs are mounting: record equity issuance/IPO supply, a 15-year/500%+ bull, and AI now cited as the #1 layoff reason.

themes.theme: Correction has technically started — shallow & rotational

insights.claim: Lance Roberts says the S&P 500 correction has technically begun: a momentum sell signal triggered, the market is working off a severe overbought condition, and price is taking out the 20-day moving average.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Confirms near-term downside bias; traders should expect further weakness and manage risk rather than chase.
insights.quote: By all intents and measures, technically speaking, yes, the correction has started.

insights.claim: Roberts expects the pullback to be a rotational 'water sloshing in the bathtub' move — capital rotating out of overbought semiconductors/mega-cap tech into lagging value/quality, producing only a 3-7% index decline rather than a deep 10-15% buy-the-dip correction.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Implies no big 'buy the dip' bargain in the index; the pain concentrates in tech while value cushions the downside.
insights.quote: the market may not actually correct in the way that a lot of people are expecting

insights.claim: Because technology and communications (Meta, Google, Nvidia and the semis) together make up ~50% of the S&P 500, and semiconductors alone are ~18%, any correction in those sectors mechanically drags the whole index lower even if money rotates into other sectors.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Index-level declines are unavoidable given concentration; cap-weight exposure carries hidden single-sector risk.
insights.quote: those two sectors so communications is meta and Google and then you know your technology sectors Nvidia all the semiconductors everything else they make up 50% of the S&P 500 index

insights.claim: The rotation is already visible: on the day of taping Micron, AMD and Intel were each down ~7% while value and quality stocks rose, and the 3x semiconductor ETF is up roughly 1,000% from where this rally started — flagging how stretched the semi trade had become.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: The unwind of the most-crowded leveraged semi trade can be violent; size and stops matter most there.
insights.quote: Micron down 7%, AMD down, uh, you know, 7%, Intel's down 7% this morning

insights.claim: Roberts expects roughly 7,300 on the S&P as minor support, with the 50-day MA (~7,150, a 38.2% retracement) and the 100-day MA (a 50% retracement) as the most realistic downside objectives — a 50% retracement of the rally implying about a 5-7% correction.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Gives concrete levels to watch for re-entry / risk sizing on the pullback.
insights.quote: the 50-day moving average, which is currently around 7150ish, is going to be your most logical next support

insights.claim: Roberts warns that after such a long complacent rally, even a normal 3-7% correction will feel like a 10-15% bear-market drop psychologically, and investors should avoid overreacting and selling out entirely.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Behavioral edge: pre-committing to not panic-sell a shallow dip protects against capitulating at the lows.
insights.quote: It's going to feel like a 10 15% correction psychologically. So, don't overreact to the correction.

insights.claim: Roberts frames the immediate-term setup as ~2% upside versus ~7% downside (a 3:1 to 4:1 risk/reward), which is why his firm trimmed high-beta tech mid-day and is adding to value — downside risk now far exceeds upside.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Asymmetric payoff argues for de-risking high-beta exposure now rather than reaching for the last few percent.
insights.quote: the upside was maybe, you know, 2% higher versus a 7% downside

insights.claim: The two-month rally off the April low is one of the largest two-month advances on record, which Roberts cites as an additional reason the move is exhausted and due to mean-revert.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Historically extreme momentum tends to be followed by give-back; supports taking profits.
insights.quote: The size of that two-month rally is one of the largest on record.

insights.claim: S&P 500 forward EPS growth estimates are being revised up toward ~30% (from below 20% a couple months ago) — a pattern of sharp upward earnings revisions that historically only occurs coming OUT of a recession (2008, dot-com, 2015-16, post-pandemic), yet is happening with no official recession.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Either earnings momentum is real and durable, or estimates are over-extended and vulnerable to a sharp reset.
insights.quote: This is something you only see coming out of recessions.

insights.claim: Roberts and Taggart argue the U.S. effectively went through a 'rolling recession' that was papered over with trillions of stimulus — Roberts' ~100-input economic composite hit recession-level readings around 2023 — which makes today's V-shaped earnings recovery a normal cyclical rebound rather than an anomaly.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Reframes the current upswing as mid-cycle recovery, supporting near-term resilience but implying an eventual reversal.
insights.quote: maybe technically we never had a recession but economically we went through an a recession

insights.claim: Taggart identifies three simultaneous stimulants 'juicing' the economy: hyperscaler capex (~$800-900B in 2026, >$1T in 2027), aggressive government deficit spending, and the One Big Beautiful Bill's 100% year-one bonus depreciation incentivizing corporate plant/equipment investment.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: These are the load-bearing pillars of growth; watch capex guidance and fiscal flows as the key macro tells.
insights.quote: there there is like multiple shots of adrenaline going into this economy

insights.claim: An economically-weighted ISM index (70% services / 30% manufacturing) has turned up from borderline-contraction, with ISM manufacturing back above 50 after nearly three years in contraction — confirming broad economic re-acceleration that supports the earnings recovery.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Hard confirmation the recovery is broad, not just AI capex; bears betting on imminent recession face a strong tape.
insights.quote: the ISM manufacturing index has been in contraction for almost three straight years. It's back into expansion territory

insights.claim: Multiple independent labor sources (Revelio Labs, Bank of America payrolls estimate, ADP) all show a sharp resurgence in hiring, corroborating the BLS non-farm payrolls uptick — Taggart's point that you can dislike the government data but several private datasets tell the same story.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: Don't trade on data-denial; corroborated labor strength reduces near-term recession odds.
insights.quote: just because you hate one indicator because it's the government... there's a lot of other indicators out there that are also telling you exactly the same thing

insights.claim: Roberts' core market philosophy: the market only prices the published data, not how you feel about its accuracy — trading on shadow-stats inflation or 'fake' CPI beliefs while the market uses official CPI impairs wealth by keeping you out of rising markets.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Discipline edge: position to the data the market actually responds to, not to a 'truer' private metric it ignores.
insights.quote: Nobody cares what you think when it comes to the financial markets... The market only cares about the data that's published.

themes.theme: Cracks in the consumer & sustainability of growth

insights.claim: Q1 GDP (~3%, likely revised to 2-2.5%) was driven by the ~15% business-investment component (data-center capex) while personal consumption (PCE, ~70% of GDP) actually contracted — the consumer is slowing with rising delinquencies and fading debt-fueled spending.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: The growth is narrowly capex-led; consumer weakness is the soft underbelly that could undercut the durability of the boom.
insights.quote: personal consumption expenditures PCE which is 70% of GDP contracted. So the consumer is slowing down.

insights.claim: Unit labor costs (largely wages) are declining sharply, which Roberts says will feed through to weaker consumption and drag CPI lower later in 2026 and into 2027 once the oil spike passes.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Disinflation thesis: falling wage costs point to lower future CPI prints and softer demand — bond-supportive, growth-cautionary.
insights.quote: unit labor costs are declining rather sharply. That's going to drag inflation lower later this year.

insights.claim: The entire bullish narrative is hostage to hyperscaler capex: if credit markets pull back and 2027 capex falls from ~$1.2T to, say, $700B, forward earnings expectations break and the whole market gets hit regardless of the rotation dynamic.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: Capex financing conditions are the single biggest tail risk; monitor AI-infrastructure credit and capex guidance closely.
insights.quote: if those if those get materially impaired, that's going to be a really big deal because so much of those earnings expectations are based on that money getting spent

insights.claim: Roberts notes strong economic growth and strong market returns are NOT the same: you can have a decent-growth year with an underperforming market depending on valuations — a caution that bullish macro green shoots don't guarantee continued index gains.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: Decouples macro optimism from equity allocation; valuation, not GDP, drives forward returns.
insights.quote: you can still have a year of pretty decent economic growth, but you could have an underperforming market

themes.theme: Late-cycle signals: IPO/equity supply & long bull

insights.claim: A historic surge in equity issuance — a record IPO pipeline (SpaceX, AI/LLM names) plus secondaries like Google's reported ~$85B raise — is, in Roberts' upcoming research, a classic late-cycle supply signal that historically precedes recessionary or crash outcomes (dot-com peak, 2020 SPAC wave).
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Massive new supply at premium prices needs demand to absorb it; when it can't, prices fall — a top-signal to respect.
insights.quote: when you have big historic spikes in equity issuance, it tends to correspond with recessionary outcomes in in the markets

insights.claim: Roberts says the wave of IPOs/issuance is insiders and private equity cashing out at premium valuations into a hot market, and he would NOT buy SpaceX on IPO day despite liking the company long-term — wait rather than pay the IPO-day premium.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Avoid IPO-day premiums on hot offerings; insider selling into strength is a distribution signal.
insights.quote: if I'm an insider and you got a market doing this, yeah, I'm looking to get out

insights.claim: Roberts notes 2008's equity-issuance surge should be excluded from the bearish historical analog because that was desperation capital-raising to avoid bankruptcy (e.g., Goldman Sachs selling preferreds to Berkshire), not opportunistic top-ticking like today.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Refines the issuance signal: today's supply is opportunistic distribution, which is the more reliable late-cycle tell.
insights.quote: they were doing equity issuance to stay alive

insights.claim: Roberts characterizes this as a 15-year, ~500-600% bull market — by magnitude of advancement the biggest ever — and stresses it will end, but momentum is hard to kill and it could run another 2-4 years before reverting.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Stay invested but vigilant; don't short a momentum bull, but recognize late-cycle positioning is warranted.
insights.quote: by far, it's the biggest bull market we've ever had

insights.claim: Roberts flags oil as the most likely 'rug pull': a supply-driven spike (oil has been flat between ~$90-100 for a month) that erodes consumer confidence, rolls into earnings expectations, and triggers a broad market sell-off — the kind of event that makes capital actually leave the 'bathtub.'
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: An oil-supply shock is the cleanest path from a shallow rotation to a real index correction; watch crude as the trigger.
insights.quote: there's a lot of complacency in the market... so it won't take a lot to do a rug pull

insights.claim: Taggart warns oil inventories have 'passed the red line' (citing imminent interview with oil expert Jeff Currie and a corroborating WSJ article), so prices could move substantially higher despite the oil market not yet pricing the Iran/Strait-of-Hormuz risk.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: Inventory drawdown is a setup for an oil squeeze; energy longs benefit, broad equities at risk if it spikes.
insights.quote: there's definitely reasons to be concerned here that oil prices could actually start going substantially higher because we've kind of passed the red line in in inventories

insights.claim: Roberts' RIA portfolio is long energy (Exxon Mobil, MLPs) and would benefit from higher oil; he's 'waiting for the oil market to tell me' to care, treating price action as the signal rather than front-running the inventory narrative.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Energy exposure is a hedge against the oil-spike scenario that would hurt the broad market.
insights.quote: we're long energy stocks. We own Exxon Mobile. We own MLPS... I would love higher oil prices because that would play out great for the portfolio

insights.claim: Liquidity / financial conditions are about as loose as they've been in a long time, sustained by bank lending surging alongside economic growth and the Fed holding lots of short-term T-bills that fund bank reserves and encourage lending — Roberts says nothing is impairing liquidity yet.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Loose liquidity is the bull's fuel; watch bank-lending slowdown, rate hikes or pricier oil as the things that would choke it.
insights.quote: liquidity is the blood of the market

insights.claim: Roberts says it would take the Fed hiking, rates rising and staying up, or much higher oil to tighten liquidity — and he does NOT expect the Fed to hike rates, keeping the liquidity backdrop supportive for now.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Absent a Fed hike or oil spike, the liquidity tide stays in — limiting downside to a shallow correction.
insights.quote: you need the Fed to hike rates... which I don't expect they will

insights.claim: Bitcoin has fallen sharply with retail enthusiasm gone quiet; the four explanations floated are a normal halving-style cyclical drawdown, Michael Saylor's Strategy (ex-MicroStrategy) selling a small amount of BTC, AI/semiconductors siphoning speculative capital, and Bitcoin signaling waning liquidity as a 'canary in the coal mine.'
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: If Bitcoin is a liquidity tell, its weakness could foreshadow tightening even while official liquidity reads loose.
insights.quote: Bitcoin's a basically a measure of liquidity and liquidity must be waning right now

insights.claim: Credit spreads remain 'dead' (complacent), having only briefly widened on the oil-driven rate spike before tightening again as oil and rates fell — Roberts reads this as no stress signal from credit markets.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Tight credit spreads remove a key recession warning; a widening would be the early sign of real trouble.
insights.quote: Yep. Dead.

insights.claim: Roberts argues the ~$2T private-credit market is too small to be 'the next subprime'; recent fund gating is a liquidity run, not credit impairment — e.g., the Blackstone BDC (BXSL) has ~98% collateralized loans and trades at a discount to NAV, which he frames as opportunity in the 'rubble.'
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Discounted, high-quality BDCs may be a contrarian buy; gating ≠ insolvency, but buyers must vet credit quality carefully.
insights.quote: gating does not necessarily mean that there's something wrong with the underlying credits

insights.claim: The labor data tells two stories: hiring is resurging but quits remain very low (signaling worker fear), the bulk of new jobs are low-wage leisure/hospitality/healthcare, and the college-graduate unemployment rate is an elevated ~5.6% — a 'low hire, no fire' market skewed to low-pay roles.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: The headline strength masks a soft, low-quality job mix — consumer spending power may be weaker than payrolls suggest.
insights.quote: The unemployment rate for college graduates right now is like 5.6%.

insights.claim: For the first time ever, Challenger Gray & Christmas data cites AI as the #1 reason for layoffs — and with AI deployment still in its earliest innings, Taggart warns the displacement effect compounds materially over the next 3-5 years.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: Structural labor displacement is now measurable; a secular headwind for entry-level employment and consumer income.
insights.quote: for the first time ever um AI is being given as the number one reason for layoffs

insights.claim: Taggart and Roberts argue AI is eroding the bottom rungs of the career ladder across every profession (bookkeeping, entry-level work), removing the development onboarding humans need — a non-obvious structural risk distinct from whole-job automation.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: Long-run productivity gains come with a hollowed-out talent pipeline; watch entry-level hiring as an AI-impact gauge.
insights.quote: those rungs of the ladder are getting removed and so you can't go from off the ladder to rung three or four

insights.claim: Roberts' RIA executed a value rotation: trimmed high-beta tech mid-day (Broadcom and other high-flyers) earlier, then added to beaten-down Walmart and Costco — citing Costco's membership-fee model as a durable cash-flow moat Walmart lacks.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Concrete value-rotation playbook: take profits in stretched tech, add quality consumer-staples with structural moats.
insights.quote: we added to Walmart. We added to Costco

insights.claim: Additional RIA swaps within the value/dividend sleeve: sold Procter & Gamble for Coca-Cola, and swapped a ~2% Duke Energy position for a ~3% American Electric Power position — all favoring better relative performance among defensive dividend names.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Shows active tilt toward dividend/defensive quality as the rotation plays out; specific name-level positioning.
insights.quote: we actually uh sold Proctor Gamble and bought Coke

insights.claim: Roberts' risk-management prescription for the correction: rebalance, cut high-beta exposure, add low-beta quality dividend stocks (terrible performers the last 3 years that protect on the downside), and add short-duration Treasuries (6-month to 1-year T-bills) to lower portfolio beta.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Actionable defensive allocation to ride out the pullback while staying invested.
insights.quote: buy some, you know, six month, one-year Treasury bills. That'll help lower the beta volatility of your portfolio.

insights.claim: On housing, Roberts says don't make a macro bet shorting subprime (don't 'be Michael Burry'); existing-home sales are at the lowest since the financial crisis and sellers are pulling listings, but a housing recession can occur without a systemic crisis or a 30% price crash.
insights.speaker: Lance Roberts
insights.so_what: Avoid the consensus housing-crash short; instead monitor and reduce portfolio risk if concerned, and shop distressed real estate selectively.
insights.quote: don't be Michael Bur and go short subprime right now
